sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.description: Define the core problem you're solving. Be specific about who is affected, what the current situation is, and why it matters now.
guiding_questions: What is the root problem — not just the symptom?
Who is affected and how severely?
What is the business or user cost if left unresolved?
What scope and constraints are you working within?
sections.placeholder: e.g. B2B customers are dropping off at Step 3 of onboarding, causing a 15% decline in activation rate in Q3. This directly impacts expansion ARR and increases CS load.

sections.title: Assumptions
sections.description: State the assumptions you're making upfront. Good answers are explicit about uncertainty — claiming certainty you don't have is a red flag.
guiding_questions: What are you treating as given that you cannot currently prove?
What data or evidence would change your recommendation?
What constraints (time, resources, tech) are you accepting as fixed?
sections.placeholder: e.g. I assume the drop-off is UX-related, not a bug. I assume engineering has ~2 sprints of capacity and that enterprise accounts behave differently from SMB.

sections.title: Goals & Success Metrics
sections.description: Define what success looks like. Tie your solution to measurable outcomes, not vague intentions.
guiding_questions: What is your primary success metric for this effort?
What guardrail metrics must not regress?
What is the target and by when?
How will you know if the solution is working in week 1? Week 4?
sections.placeholder: e.g. Primary: activation rate back to 52%+ within 30 days. Guardrail: support ticket volume does not increase. Instrumentation: track Step 3 completion funnel.

sections.title: User / Customer Perspective
sections.description: Show that you understand the user's experience and needs behind the problem. The best solutions are grounded in who you're designing for.
guiding_questions: Who is the primary user and what are they trying to accomplish?
Where does the current experience break down for them?
Are there meaningfully different user segments with different needs?
What would delight them vs. just fix the problem?
sections.placeholder: e.g. Enterprise admins want to onboard their team quickly; confusion at Step 3 likely stems from unclear permission settings for multi-seat setups vs. solo users.

sections.title: Proposed Solution
sections.description: Describe your recommendation clearly. A clear, explicit recommendation is required — don't bury the lead or end with 'it depends' without a follow-through decision.
guiding_questions: What is your recommendation, in one sentence?
What are the key components of the solution?
What did you consider and reject, and why?
What would you do in Week 1 vs. later phases?
sections.placeholder: e.g. Recommendation: ship a targeted fix to the Step 3 permission UI for enterprise accounts, with a fallback in-app guide. This addresses the highest-impact segment first within existing sprint capacity.

sections.title: Prioritization & Trade-offs
sections.description: Explain what you're choosing not to do and why those trade-offs are acceptable. Good PM judgment is visible in what you deprioritize, not just what you choose.
guiding_questions: What alternatives did you weigh?
What is the opportunity cost of your choice?
What are you deprioritizing and why is that acceptable right now?
How did you balance speed, scope, and quality?
sections.placeholder: e.g. I chose a targeted fix over a full onboarding redesign. The redesign would take 6+ weeks and risk scope creep. The quick fix unblocks ~60% of affected users in one sprint.

sections.title: Execution Plan
sections.description: Describe how you'll deliver the solution. Be realistic about scope, sequencing, and who needs to be involved.
guiding_questions: What are the key milestones and timeline?
What dependencies and risks could block delivery?
Who needs to be involved (eng, design, CS, legal, etc.)?
What's the rollout approach — phased, feature flag, full release?
sections.placeholder: e.g. Week 1: discovery + design mockup. Week 2–3: build + QA. Week 4: staged rollout to 10% of enterprise accounts behind a flag. Week 5: full rollout if metrics hold.

sections.title: Risks & Mitigations
sections.description: Identify what could go wrong and how you'd handle it. Proactively naming risks (and mitigations) is a strong signal of execution maturity.
guiding_questions: What are the top 2–3 risks of this approach?
What is the mitigation or contingency for each?
What is your rollback plan if the solution backfires?
What would trigger you to change course mid-execution?
sections.placeholder: e.g. Risk: the fix inadvertently breaks a legacy permission flow for a small enterprise segment. Mitigation: dark-launch behind a feature flag and validate with 2 pilot accounts first. Rollback: toggle the flag off.

sections.title: Reflection & Next Steps
sections.description: Show self-awareness about uncertainty and what you'd learn or validate post-launch. This section demonstrates intellectual honesty and continuous improvement mindset.
guiding_questions: What would you validate in the first 2 weeks post-launch?
What is your biggest remaining uncertainty, and how would you resolve it?
What would you do differently with 2x the time or budget?
What surprised you or what would you investigate further?
sections.placeholder: e.g. If activation improves for SMB but not enterprise, I'd investigate whether the root cause differs by segment and reconsider whether the fix was too narrowly scoped.

what_system_expects.summary: We evaluate the quality of your thinking, not whether you followed a rigid structure. A strong answer is specific, honest about trade-offs, and clear about what you know vs. assume. Depth over breadth: a focused, well-reasoned response scores higher than covering every section shallowly.
expectations: Make a clear recommendation — don't leave it open-ended.
Be explicit about your assumptions and what would change your mind.
Connect your decision to at least one measurable outcome.
Acknowledge what you're deprioritizing and why it's an acceptable trade-off.
Be realistic about execution — timelines, dependencies, and risks matter.
Avoid claiming certainty you don't have; show structured thinking under uncertainty.
always_expected: Some acknowledgment of constraints or trade-offs
valued_but_not_required: Formal section headers matching this template
Long responses — concise and precise is better than exhaustive

scoring_dimensions.label: Problem Framing
scoring_dimensions.description: How well you define the problem, its scope, and the user or business impact. Strong answers reframe the prompt with sharper insight rather than restating it.
good_signals: Explains who is impacted and how severely

scoring_dimensions.label: Decision Quality & Trade-offs
scoring_dimensions.description: Whether your recommendation is clear and whether you've considered alternatives and their costs. Picking the right option isn't enough — explaining why earns higher scores.
good_signals: Names and explains at least one rejected alternative

scoring_dimensions.label: Evidence & Assumptions
scoring_dimensions.description: Whether you're explicit about what you know vs. assume, and how you'd validate those assumptions. Don't claim certainty you don't have.
good_signals: Names what data would validate or invalidate them
Describes a discovery plan for high-uncertainty decisions

scoring_dimensions.label: Execution Realism
scoring_dimensions.description: Whether your plan is feasible given the scenario's constraints — timeline, team, dependencies, and risk. A great strategy with an impossible execution plan scores lower.
good_signals: Proposes a phased or staged rollout
Includes a rollback or mitigation plan

scoring_dimensions.label: Metrics & Success Criteria
scoring_dimensions.description: Whether you define what success looks like — with specific, measurable outcomes. Guardrail metrics (what must not worsen) are a bonus.
good_signals: Defines a primary KPI with a target
Sets a timeframe for the expected outcome
Mentions at least one guardrail metric

scale.1: Mentioned but vague — no reasoning, or major gaps
scale.2: Basic structure but incomplete trade-offs, assumptions, or feasibility
scale.3: Solid — clear recommendation with reasoning, addresses constraints
scale.4: Strong — good prioritization, risk mitigation, anticipates stakeholder concerns
scale.5: Excellent — expert-level judgment across all dimensions; a 5 is rare

scoring_notes: Weights vary by case type and role. Some scenarios emphasize execution depth; others test strategic judgment.
If a scenario includes stakeholder roles, alignment with their concerns is also factored into the score.
Strong answers are not just 'correct' — they are well-reasoned, explicit about assumptions, and aware of trade-offs.
Absence of section headers is not penalized. Substance matters more than format.
A response without a clear recommendation cannot score above 2, regardless of other strengths.

associate_pm.label: Associate PM
associate_pm.focus_note: At this level, evaluators prioritize clarity of thinking, structured communication, and sound prioritization instincts. Show that you can break down a problem and reason through it step by step. You don't need to have seen this exact problem before — the goal is demonstrating a replicable thinking process.

section_hints.proposed_solution: Lead with a clear recommendation. It's okay to scope down to a simpler solution if you explain your reasoning clearly.
section_hints.tradeoffs: Show that you considered at least two options and can explain why you chose one over the other — even briefly.
section_hints.execution_plan: Even a rough timeline with 2–3 key milestones demonstrates execution awareness. You don't need full sprint planning.
section_hints.reflection: Acknowledging what you'd still want to learn shows intellectual honesty — this is valued at all levels.

what_we_look_for: Can you frame a problem clearly without a perfectly structured prompt?
Do you make a decision, or do you hedge without follow-through?
Are you honest about what you don't know?
Can you identify at least one concrete success metric?

senior_pm.label: Senior PM
senior_pm.focus_note: At this level, evaluators look for strategic judgment, stakeholder alignment, and execution depth. You're expected to navigate ambiguity confidently, make strong opinionated calls, and explicitly balance short-term delivery with long-term product health.

section_hints.problem_statement: Go beyond restating the prompt — bring a sharper hypothesis, segmentation insight, or strategic framing.
section_hints.tradeoffs: Address opportunity cost at the portfolio level, not just within this feature. Why this vs. other priorities?
section_hints.execution_plan: Show awareness of organizational complexity: dependencies, change management, rollout sequencing for different customer segments.
section_hints.reflection: Proactively identify what assumptions you'd test, how you'd build conviction, and what a 'version 2' might look like.

what_we_look_for: Is the framing crisp and insightful beyond what was handed to you?
Do trade-off discussions account for strategic alignment and opportunity cost?
Is the execution plan realistic and multi-dimensional?

ai_pm.label: AI Product Manager
ai_pm.focus_note: AI product decisions carry unique risks and constraints. Evaluators look for awareness of model limitations, evaluation quality, human-in-the-loop design, and the distinction between what AI can do technically vs. what it should do in your specific product context.

section_hints.assumptions: Be explicit about model constraints — accuracy, latency, hallucination risk, data availability. Don't assume AI capabilities that aren't established for your context.
section_hints.proposed_solution: Describe the AI's role in the product and what happens when it fails or returns low-confidence outputs. Human-in-the-loop design is expected.
section_hints.risks: AI-specific risks to address: bias, model drift, regulatory/compliance exposure, edge cases, adversarial inputs. Name at least one explicitly.
section_hints.goals_and_metrics: Offline model metrics (precision, recall, F1) are not sufficient — define product-level success metrics and how you'd evaluate AI impact in production.

what_we_look_for: Do you distinguish between what AI can do technically vs. what's safe and appropriate to ship?
Do you define evaluation quality, monitoring, and human review requirements?
Are you aware of model drift, data quality issues, and feedback loop risks?
Do you address at least one regulatory, ethical, or compliance surface area?

growth_pm.label: Growth PM
growth_pm.focus_note: Growth problems require hypothesis-driven thinking, funnel fluency, and rigorous experiment design. Evaluators look for quantitative reasoning, clear metrics hierarchies, and an understanding of how growth levers interact.

section_hints.problem_statement: Frame the problem in terms of funnel stages or growth accounting (acquisition, activation, retention, revenue, referral).
section_hints.proposed_solution: Define the growth lever you're pulling and the hypothesis behind it. What behavior change are you expecting?
section_hints.goals_and_metrics: Define experiment design if applicable — sample size, duration, success threshold, and any confounders to control for.
section_hints.risks: Address experiment validity risks: novelty effect, survivorship bias, network effects, instrumentation error.

what_we_look_for: Is the growth lever clearly identified and justified?
Is the experiment or rollout designed to produce valid, actionable signal?
Are metrics defined with baselines, targets, and guardrails?
Are compounding or systemic effects of the growth move considered?

example_good_outline.title: Example: Strong Associate PM Response Outline
example_good_outline.note: This is a fictional example of how a well-structured response might look. Substance matters more than format.
example_good_outline.content:
**Recommendation:** Run a targeted experiment to fix the Step 3 permission UI for enterprise accounts, rolled out behind a feature flag.

**Why this, not a full redesign:** A full onboarding redesign would take 6–8 weeks. The data points to Step 3 as the primary drop-off point for enterprise accounts specifically. Fixing one step first lets us validate the root cause with lower risk and faster learning.

**Assumptions:** I'm assuming the drop is UX-related, not a backend bug (to be confirmed with eng). I'm also assuming enterprise and SMB users have different needs at this step.

**Success metric:** Activation rate for enterprise accounts returns to 52%+ within 30 days of full rollout. Guardrail: support ticket volume doesn't increase.

**Plan:** Week 1 — design mockup + validate with 2 enterprise customers. Week 2–3 — build + QA. Week 4 — 10% staged rollout behind a flag. Week 5 — full rollout if metrics hold. Rollback: toggle flag off.

**Risks:** If the fix only helps SMB accounts, I'd revisit scope. If engineering bandwidth shrinks, I'd cut the in-app guide from scope and ship just the permission flow fix.
